title: Automated Summary Pitfalls
concept: ROUGE can score grammatically broken summaries highly if they contain the correct words; human validation remains essential. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Transformer Foundations.'

exercises.prompt: What is ROUGE in the context of text summarization?

options.text: A tool that automatically writes summaries for you.
An automated metric that scores summaries based on matching words.
A human editor who checks summaries for grammatical mistakes.

exercises.explain: ROUGE is an automated metric that measures word overlap between a generated summary and a reference summary.

cards.title: The Word Salad Trap
cards.body: Have you ever read a summary that used all the right words but made absolutely zero sense? Computer programs like ROUGE can easily be fooled by these 'word salads' because they can't actually read like we do!

cards.title: The Limits of ROUGE
cards.body: Automated metrics like ROUGE are great for quick tests, but they have a major pitfall: they only count matching words. They do not understand grammar, flow, or facts. This means a summary can get a perfect score even if it reads like complete gibberish!

cards.title: The Spelling Bee Judge
cards.body: Imagine a spelling bee where the judge only checks if you used the right letters, regardless of their order! If you spell 'cat' as 'act', you still get full points. That is how ROUGE works. It looks for matching words but ignores whether the sentence actually makes sense to a human reader.

cards.title: Spotting the Gibberish
cards.body:
Compare these two summary attempts for a story about a dog:

Summary A: 'Dog park ran happy yesterday.' (High ROUGE score due to key words, but terrible grammar!)

Summary B: 'The dog had a wonderful time at the park.' (Good grammar and clear meaning.)

ROUGE might rate them similarly, but a human helper instantly spots that Summary A is broken.

cards.title: Myth: High Score = High Quality
cards.body: People often think that a high ROUGE score means a summary is perfect and ready for users. In reality, a summary can score highly while being grammatically broken or even factually incorrect. Human validation is always essential!

cards.title: Two Ways of Looking
cards.body: Let's compare how a computer metric and a human reader evaluate the exact same poorly-written summary.

left.text: Looks only at matching words. Gives a HIGH score because 'dog', 'park', and 'ran' are present.

right.text: Looks at grammar and flow. Rejects the summary because it is written in a scrambled, broken way.

exercises.prompt: Why might a grammatically broken summary get a high ROUGE score?

options.text: It uses advanced artificial intelligence to correct its own errors.
It contains the correct key words, even if they are in a scrambled order.
It matches the exact length of the original text.
It is written by a human expert instead of a computer.

exercises.explain: ROUGE looks at overlapping words, not how they are structured. A jumbled list of key words will still score highly.

exercises.prompt: True or False: ROUGE can automatically detect if a summary has bad grammar.
exercises.explain: ROUGE only counts word matches; it does not analyze grammatical structure or coherence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule of summary validation.
exercises.sentence: While ROUGE helps check word overlap, we still need human validation to ensure the summary is readable.
exercises.explain: Because ROUGE only matches words, we need real humans to check if the summary actually reads well and makes sense.

exercises.prompt: Two AI models summarized a recipe. Which one is showing a ROUGE loophole failure?

options.text: AI Model A: 'Bake the chocolate cake at 350 degrees for 30 minutes.'
AI Model B: 'Cake bake chocolate oven minutes 30 cook heat.'
AI Model C: 'This recipe explains how to bake a cake.'

exercises.explain: AI Model B created a meaningless list of words, which is a classic ROUGE loophole, while AI Model A wrote a proper, coherent sentence.

exercises.prompt: Why can we NOT rely solely on ROUGE scores to judge if a summary is good?

options.text: ROUGE automatically rewrites broken sentences to fix their grammar.
ROUGE measures how much humans enjoy reading the generated text.
ROUGE can give high scores to meaningless word salads that contain the key terms.
ROUGE is the only test needed to guarantee high-quality summary outputs.

exercises.explain: ROUGE only counts word overlaps and completely misses bad grammar, repetition, or nonsensical phrasing. Always back up automated scores with human feedback! Next up: Transformer Foundations.
